# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Envoy proxy configuration for HTTP traffic routing. Define listeners on port 80, clusters for backend services, and routes to match requests. Configure connection pooling and health checks for upstream services. This is for our API gateway proxy.
# Category: envoy_no_tls

envoy_proxy:
  listeners:
    - name: listener-1
      address: any
      port: 80
      
  clusters:
     cluster-backendA:   #cluster for backend service A's upstream server (ip or hostname)
         endpoints:         
           - "host: backendsvra"    # IP of the BackEnd Service B on which we want to route our requests. Make sure it is correctly configured in envoy file 1 and below configuration matches with backend service A's port if different use correct environment variables like ${envoy_serviceA}
           - "host: backendsvrb"   # IP of the BackEnd Service B on which we want to route our requests. Make sure it is correctly configured in envoy file 1 and below configuration matches with backend service A's port if different use correct environment variables like ${envoy_serviceA}
         cluster-priority:    20   # Assign a higher priority for this upstream, which should be done on purpose as we have clusters B in next line. 1=highest precedence (Default), Higher numbers = lower prececedence - only if same cost/load can't decide
         load_assignment:      round-robin    # Load balancing method between the endpoints to distribute incoming traffic evenly among them..round ron, least connections or ip_hash — depends on your use case. default is randomly and consistent by name if no specific parameters are provided in upstream cluster configuration
         
     cluster-backendB:   #cluster for backend service B's UpStream server (ip/hostname)      //In the same format as above - assign a priority based upon load, use your backendsvra and backserrb accordingly.  If different IP or hostnames are used in next block configuration make sure they match with envoy_serviceA
         endpoints:          # Endpoint list of backend service B's upstream server(ip/hostname) to which requests will be directed, Replace ${envoy_svrb} and similarly for the other backends.  Use your respective IP or hostnames here accordingly with env variables when required in next block configuration
         cluster-priority:    10   # Prioritizing this upstream by a higher number as above (2) - should be used on purpose, assigning priority to services which are slower and need more resources. If your environment can't decide the load balance then define accordingly with different priorities like ${envoy_priorityA}
         health-checks:        #Health check configuration for backend service A  //Use IP or hostname of endpoints as required in next block to enable it and set up specific parameters (default is HTTP /2 GET)   e.g., http://$BACKENDABACKUP/healthcheck, https:/backendbport
           - name: health-1    # Health check parameter's unique identifier that must match with the config file of envoy_proxy section above  //Should be same as provided in previous block configuration when it was enabled. Use ${envox_{name}} to enable this and set up correct parameters here e,g., http://$BACKENDABACKUP/healthcheck
               interval:   60s   # Health check's parameter - Interval (sec)  //This should be defined as per requirement based on your use case. default is ${envoy_interval} for health checks enabled above in next block configuration when it was set, if you want then make sure to provide value and same goes with the interval of each one
               timeout:   1s   # Health check's parameter - Timeout (sec)  //Same as before but should be used based on your use case. default is ${envoy_timeout} for health checks enabled above in next block configuration when it was set, if you want then make sure to provide value and same goes with the timeout of each one
               healthy:   2   # Health check's parameter - Required Successful Checks  //In case 'healthy': The number of successful HTTP responses that should be seen as "Healthy". default is ${envoy_successive} for health checks enabled above in next block configuration when it was set, if you want then make sure to provide value and same goes with the healthy count.
               unhealthy:  1  # Health check's parameter - Required Failed Checks //Same as before but should be used based on your use case where 'unHealthy': The number of failed HTTP responses that are required from a client or system to report back an "Un-Health". default is ${envoy_failed} for health checks enabled above in next block configuration when it was set, if you want then make sure to provide value and same goes with the unhealthly count.
      route:   #Route Configuration  //Assuming there are no routes configured till now so far - Make them as per requirement based on how many clusters have endpoints (Clusters A & B), each one has a specific path(i.e., /route1, etc.), method type like GET/POST and other configuration if required
         cluster:   #Cluster Name for Route Configuration e.g “cluster-backendA” which should be assigned in previous block when creating route configs above with http methods as well (GET/ POST). Replace ${envoy_route} to match your use case here and assign correct names accordingly
           - name: "/*"   #If no specific path is provided then ‘*’ will mean all paths. This must be in form of a string which matches above block configuration if required, otherwise it would throw an error while creating route configs for cluster-backendA as per given code snippet
             weight:   10   #Route's parameter - Weight (default is 1). Replace ${envoy_weight} with correct values that suit your use case. default value will be used if not provided above in next block configuration when it was set, otherwise then provide the same here as per requirement
